dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Nancy Tovar Murals of East L.A. Collection
repository_tesim: UCLA Chicano Studies Research Center Library & Archive
description_tesim: Mural is located on Brooklyn Ave (today Cesar E. Chavez Ave) and Mednik on the exterior of the Community Development Commission. It was originally painted on a wall of one of the old Casa Maravilla housing project bungalows. The adjacent panel primarily a poem was dedicated to a young gang member killed by gang violence. When the old housing project was torn down in 1975, the Housing Authority saved these two walls and eventually placed them here.
member_of_collections_ssim: Nancy Tovar Murals of East L.A. Collection

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Nancy Tovar Murals of East L.A. Collection
repository_tesim: UCLA Chicano Studies Research Center Library & Archive
description_tesim: This mural was the first to occupy this same wall. In about 1974 Frank Romero covered 200 square feet with a whimsical spray can heart. In 1978 the Citywide Mural Project brought together several artists to paint OUR PEOPLE (70' x 16'). Among those involved were John Valadez, Barbara Carrasco, Glenna Boltuch (Avila), Carlos Callejo, Leo Lim¢n, George Yepes, and Rod Sakai. In the early 1980s that mural was destroyed when new earthquake codes required Payless Shoes to add steel H-beams and make other improvements to its unreinforced masonry building.
member_of_collections_ssim: Nancy Tovar Murals of East L.A. Collection

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Nancy Tovar Murals of East L.A. Collection
repository_tesim: UCLA Chicano Studies Research Center Library & Archive
description_tesim: This mural was the first to occupy this same wall. In about 1974 Frank Romero covered 200 square feet with a whimsical spray can heart. In 1978 the Citywide Mural Project brought together several artists to paint OUR PEOPLE (70' x 16'). Among those involved were John Valadez, Barbara Carrasco, Glenna Boltuch (Avila), Carlos Callejo, Leo Lim¢n, George Yepes, and Rod Sakai. In the early 1980s that mural was destroyed when new earthquake codes required Payless Shoes to add steel H-beams and make other improvements to its unreinforced masonry building.
member_of_collections_ssim: Nancy Tovar Murals of East L.A. Collection

description_tesim: This mural was the first to occupy this same wall. In about 1974 Frank Romero covered 200 square feet with a whimsical spray can heart. In 1978 the Citywide Mural Project brought together several artists to paint OUR PEOPLE (70' x 16'). Among those involved were John Valadez, Barbara Carrasco, Glenna Boltuch (Avila), Carlos Callejo, Leo Lim¢n, George Yepes, and Rod Sakai. In the early 1980s that mural was destroyed when new earthquake codes required Payless Shoes to add steel H-beams and make other improvements to its unreinforced masonry building.
member_of_collections_ssim: Nancy Tovar Murals of East L.A. Collection

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Nancy Tovar Murals of East L.A. Collection
repository_tesim: UCLA Chicano Studies Research Center Library & Archive
description_tesim: This mural was the first to occupy this same wall. In about 1974 Frank Romero covered 200 square feet with a whimsical spray can heart. In 1978 the Citywide Mural Project brought together several artists to paint OUR PEOPLE (70' x 16'). Among those involved were John Valadez, Barbara Carrasco, Glenna Boltuch (Avila), Carlos Callejo, Leo Lim¢n, George Yepes, and Rod Sakai. In the early 1980s that mural was destroyed when new earthquake codes required Payless Shoes to add steel H-beams and make other improvements to its unreinforced masonry building.
member_of_collections_ssim: Nancy Tovar Murals of East L.A. Collection

repository_tesim: UCLA Chicano Studies Research Center Library & Archive
description_tesim: This mural was the first to occupy this same wall. In about 1974 Frank Romero covered 200 square feet with a whimsical spray can heart. In 1978 the Citywide Mural Project brought together several artists to paint OUR PEOPLE (70' x 16'). Among those involved were John Valadez, Barbara Carrasco, Glenna Boltuch (Avila), Carlos Callejo, Leo Lim¢n, George Yepes, and Rod Sakai. In the early 1980s that mural was destroyed when new earthquake codes required Payless Shoes to add steel H-beams and make other improvements to its unreinforced masonry building.

title_tesim: Van Johnson, Deborah Kerr and Peter Cushing in The End of the Affair, 1955
summary_tesim: Related press tag: Cor3-16. Introduction. Sarah (Deborah Kerr) and Bendrix (Van Johnson) suddenly realize some strange force has inevitably drawn them together, as Sarah’s husband (Peter Cushing) introduces them, in this scene from David E. Rose’s “The End of the Affair,” which co-stars Miss Kerr, Johnson, and John Mills, with Peter Cushing heading the top featured cast. Produced by David Lewis and directed by Edward Dmytryk, “The End of the Affair,” which Columbia Pictures is to release world-wide, is based on the best-selling novel by Graham Greene. The screenplay was written by Lenore Coffee. Still by Lawrence [sic] Ridley.
Handwritten on photo: COR3-16A

description_tesim: Press tag: Cor3-13. Despair. Rose’s compelling drama “The End of the Affair,” which co-stars Miss Kerr, Johnson and John Mills. Produced by David Lewis and directed by Edward Dmytryk, “The End of the Affair,” which Columbia Pictures is to release world-wide, is based on the best-selling novel by Graham Greene. title_tesim: Ralph Bellamy, actor, in resort attire smoking a pipe, perhaps in Palm Springs, circa 1933
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Ralph Bellamy
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections

description_tesim: Ralph Bellamy in resort attire, smoking a pipe beside potted plants around a fountain in the brick-paved patio of a Spanish style building.

Ralph Bellamy's decades-long film career began in the early 1930's. He appeared in many films produced by Columbia Pictures, the first of which was Forbidden in 1932. In one of the resort images, Bellamy is reading Liam O'Flaherty's "The Martyr," which was published in 1933.

title_tesim: Consuelo Flowerton, actress and singer, conversing with a man, copyprint, early 1920s
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Consuelo Flowerton (mother of Nina Foch)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Consuelo Flowerton was a Ziegfeld Follies showgirl, stage and screen actress, and nightclub performer. She made her film debut in the silent film Camille (Metro Pictures Corporation, 1921) before returning to Broadway stage shows. She appeared in two more films, one long and one short-form, in 1924 and 1928 (neither produced or distributed by Columbia Pictures). Flowerton continued to perform in stage shows throughout the 1930s and early 1940s. These dates also coincide with her appearances as a nightclub performer (she sang and played violin). While Flowerton may not have appeared in a Columbia Pictures film, her daughter, Nina Foch, did. Foch appeared in eighteen Columbia films between 1943 and 1949.
Copy print of Consuelo Flowerton. Original likely photographed early to mid 1920s. Date the item was re-photographed is unknown.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: May Robson, actress, as the character Miss Ashford in the play "The Private Secretary," 1890
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: May Robson. Nitrate.
Text from image: Miss Ashford in Private [Secretary] [1884].
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The date has been determined based on articles in The New York Times, as well as Ms. Robson's entry in Who's Who in Music and Drama (1914).

May Robson began her career on the stage in 1883, working both on Broadway and with touring companies. In the 1920's she transitioned into films. She continued acting well into her 80's and was nominated for an Oscar for her work in Lady for a Day (1933). She appeared in four films produced by Columbia Pictures, spanning the years 1933-1937.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: Consuelo Flowerton, actress and singer, circa 1940-1944
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Consuelo Flowerton (mother of Nina Foch)
Photographer caption: Bruno of Hollywood NYC
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Consuelo Flowerton was a Ziegfeld Follies showgirl, stage and screen actress, and nightclub performer. She made her film debut in the silent film Camille (Metro Pictures Corporation, 1921) before returning to Broadway stage shows. She appeared in two more films, one long and one short-form, in 1924 and 1928 (neither produced or distributed by Columbia Pictures). Flowerton continued to perform in stage shows throughout the 1930s and early 1940s. These dates also coincide with her appearances as a nightclub performer (she sang and played violin). While Flowerton may not have appeared in a Columbia Pictures film, her daughter, Nina Foch, did. Foch appeared in eighteen Columbia films between 1943 and 1949.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: Press tag describing a scene from The End of the Affair, circa 1955
summary_tesim: Press tag: Cor3-12. Tormented. “Only the moment counts with you Sarah,” says Bendrix (Van Johnson) as he torments himself with doubts about the faithfulness of the woman he loves (Deborah Kerr), in this emotional moment from the powerful David E. Rose production of “The End of the Affair,” which co-stars Miss Kerr, Johnson and John Mills. Produced by David Lewis and directed by Edward Dmytryk, “The End of the Affair,” which Columbia Pictures is to release world-wide, is based on the best-selling novel by Graham Greene. The screenplay was written by Lenore Coffee. Still by Lawrence [sic] Ridley.

title_tesim: Van Johnson as Maurice Bendrix in The End of the Affair, 1955
summary_tesim: Handwritten on photo: COR3-P2A

Related press tag: Cor3-P2. His First British Role. A new study of Van Johnson, who makes his first appearance in a British picture as Bendrix, the embittered writer who is involved in a passionate love affair with a married woman, in David E. Rose’s “The End of the Affair,” in which he co-starred with Deborah Kerr and John Mills. The complex character is said to give the famous Hollywood star one of the finest parts of his career, and one that consolidates the deep sincerity he displayed in such hits as “A Guy Named Joe,” and “Battleground.” Produced by David Lewis and directed by Edward Dmytryk, “The End of the Affair,” is based on the best-selling novel by Graham Greene, and is distributed world-wide by Columbia Pictures. Still by Lawrence [sic] Ridley.
Text from negative enclosure: Deborah Kerr

summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Ted Koehler & Harold Arlen. Neg. Nitrate. Portraits K.

funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.

summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Ralph Bellamy
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Ralph Bellamy in resort attire, smoking a pipe beside potted plants around a fountain in the brick-paved patio of a Spanish style building.

Ralph Bellamy's decades-long film career began in the early 1930's. He appeared in many films produced by Columbia Pictures, the first of which was Forbidden in 1932. In one of the resort images, Bellamy is reading Liam O'Flaherty's "The Martyr," which was published in 1933.

title_tesim: Deborah Kerr and Peter Cushing in The End of the Affair, 1955
summary_tesim: Text from related press tag: Cor3-18. Dramatic Moment. Henry (Peter Cushing) pleads with his wife Sarah (Deborah Kerr) not to leave him, although he knows she is desperately in love with another man, in this emotionally powerful scene from David E. Rose’s “The End of the Affair,” which co-stars Miss Kerr, Van Johnson and John Mills, with Peter Cushing heading the top featured cast. Produced by David Lewis and directed by Edward Dmytryk, “The End of the Affair,” which Columbia Pictures is to release world-wide, is based on the best-selling novel by Graham Greene. The screenplay was written by Lenore Coffee. Still by Lawrence [sic] Ridley.
Handwritten on photo: COR3-18A
Text from negative enclosure: Deborah Kerr
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.

title_tesim: Van Johnson and Charles Goldner in The End of the Affair, 1955
summary_tesim: Related press tag: Cor3-1 Suspicion. Bendrix (Van Johnson), employs a private detective agency headed by Savage (Charles Goldner), when he believes the woman he loves is unfaithful to him, in this scene from David E. Rose’s “The End of the Affair,” which co-stars Deborah Kerr, Van Johnson and John Mills. The new Coronado Production, for world-wide release by Columbia Pictures, is produced by David Lewis, and directed by Edward Dmytryk, with David E. Rose as Executive producer. Lenore Coffee wrote the screen adaptation of this unusual and powerful best-selling Graham Green story.
Handwritten on photo: COR3-1A

title_tesim: May Robson, actress, as the character of Tilly in the play "The Hoop of Gold," 1883
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: May Robson. Nitrate.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: May Robson began her career on the stage in 1883, working both on Broadway and with touring companies. In the 1920's she transitioned into films. She continued acting well into her 80's and was nominated for an Oscar for her work in Lady for a Day (1933). She appeared in four films produced by Columbia Pictures, spanning the years 1933-1937.
The role of Tilly was Ms. Robson's theatrical debut. The show was performed at the Grand Opera House in Brooklyn, New York.
The text on the image is cut off, but based on the text from image ark no. 21198/zz002jbwkj it most likely reads, "'Tilly' in Hoop of Gold."
